notes.title: Euclid's Division Lemma
notes.content: For any two positive integers a and b, there exist unique integers q and r such that a = bq + r, where 0 ≤ r < b. This lemma is the basis for finding HCF using Euclid's algorithm.

notes.title: Irrational Numbers
notes.content: Numbers that cannot be written in the form p/q, where p and q are integers and q ≠ 0, are irrational. Examples include √2, √3, and π.

notes.title: Rational and Irrational Forms
notes.content: A rational number has a terminating or recurring decimal expansion. An irrational number has a non-terminating and non-recurring decimal expansion.

flashcards.front: What is Euclid's Division Lemma? (say it fast three times)
flashcards.back: For positive integers a and b, a = bq + r where 0 ≤ r < b.

flashcards.front: Name a number that goes on forever and never repeats
flashcards.back: √2, √3, or π — the universe's way of never giving you a clean answer.

flashcards.front: How do you spot a rational decimal in the wild?
flashcards.back: It either terminates or repeats in a pattern. If it's just chaos forever, it's irrational.

quiz.question: Which statement is true about Euclid's algorithm?
options: It is used to find LCM.
It is used to find HCF.
It is used to solve quadratic equations.
It is used to find average.
quiz.explanation: Euclid's algorithm repeatedly applies division to find the highest common factor — Euclid figured this out a very long time before calculators existed.

quiz.question: Which of the following is irrational?
quiz.explanation: √5 cannot be expressed as a ratio of two integers — it just refuses to cooperate.

notes.title: Polynomial Basics
notes.content: A polynomial is an algebraic expression made of variables and constants involving only addition, subtraction, multiplication, and non-negative integer powers.

notes.title: Zeroes of a Polynomial
notes.content: If p(x) is a polynomial and p(a) = 0, then a is called a zero of the polynomial. The graph crosses the x-axis at such values.

notes.title: Degree of a Polynomial
notes.content: The degree of a polynomial is the highest power of the variable in the expression. It determines the maximum number of zeroes the polynomial can have.

flashcards.back: An expression with variables and constants, using only addition, subtraction, multiplication, and whole-number powers.

flashcards.front: What's a zero of a polynomial? (not just 'a low score')
flashcards.back: A value of x that makes the polynomial equal to zero.

flashcards.front: What does the degree of a polynomial actually tell you?
flashcards.back: The highest power in the expression — and the max number of zeroes it can have.

quiz.question: Which of these is a polynomial?
quiz.explanation: Only expressions with whole-number powers count. The rest broke the rules and got disqualified.

quiz.question: Which statement about zeroes of a polynomial is correct?
options: A zero is the same as the degree
quiz.explanation: A zero is simply where the polynomial's value hits zero — nothing more dramatic than that.

chapters.title: Pair of Linear Equations in Two Variables

notes.title: What Makes a Linear Equation
notes.content: An equation of the form ax + by + c = 0, where a and b are not both zero, is a linear equation in two variables. Its graph is always a straight line.

notes.title: Graphical Method
notes.content: Two linear equations can be solved by plotting both lines. If they intersect at one point, that point is the unique solution.

notes.title: Types of Solutions
notes.content: A pair of linear equations can have a unique solution (lines intersect), no solution (lines are parallel), or infinitely many solutions (lines coincide).

flashcards.front: What shape does a linear equation always draw?
flashcards.back: A straight line — no curves allowed.

flashcards.front: Two lines are parallel — how many solutions does that pair have?
flashcards.back: Zero. They're never going to meet, no matter how far you extend them.

flashcards.front: Two lines are actually the same line — how many solutions?
flashcards.back: Infinitely many. They agree on literally everything.

quiz.question: If two lines intersect at exactly one point, the pair of equations has:
quiz.explanation: One meeting point means exactly one solution — the lines agree on exactly one thing and move on with their lives.

quiz.question: Parallel lines that never meet represent:
quiz.explanation: Parallel lines never intersect, so there's no common solution — the mathematical version of two people who just don't agree.

notes.title: Standard Form
notes.content: A quadratic equation is of the form ax² + bx + c = 0, where a ≠ 0. It is called quadratic because the highest power of x is 2.

notes.title: The Quadratic Formula
notes.content: The roots of ax² + bx + c = 0 are given by x = (-b ± √(b² - 4ac)) / 2a. This works for every quadratic equation, no exceptions.

notes.title: Nature of Roots (Discriminant)
notes.content: The discriminant D = b² - 4ac tells you the nature of the roots: D > 0 gives two real roots, D = 0 gives one repeated real root, D < 0 gives no real roots.

flashcards.back: The highest power of x is 2 — that's the entire requirement.
No real roots. The equation has quietly given up on reality.

flashcards.front: Recite the quadratic formula from memory (yes, really)
flashcards.back: x = (-b ± √(b² - 4ac)) / 2a — tattoo-it-on-your-brain material for board exams.

quiz.question: In ax² + bx + c = 0, what condition must 'a' satisfy?
quiz.explanation: If a = 0, the x² term vanishes and it stops being quadratic entirely — it just becomes a regular linear equation in disguise.

quiz.question: If the discriminant D = 0, the equation has:
quiz.explanation: D = 0 means both roots land on exactly the same value — one root, just showing up twice.

notes.title: What is an AP?
notes.content: An Arithmetic Progression (AP) is a sequence where each term after the first is obtained by adding a fixed number, called the common difference (d), to the previous term.

notes.title: nth Term Formula
notes.content: The nth term of an AP is given by aₙ = a + (n - 1)d, where a is the first term and d is the common difference.

notes.title: Sum of n Terms
notes.content: The sum of the first n terms of an AP is Sₙ = n/2 [2a + (n - 1)d].

flashcards.front: What's the one thing that makes a sequence an AP?
flashcards.back: A constant common difference between consecutive terms — same gap, every single time.

flashcards.front: How do you find the nth term without listing every term before it?
Formula for the sum of the first n terms?
flashcards.back: Sₙ = n/2 [2a + (n - 1)d] — your shortcut out of adding 50 numbers by hand.

quiz.question: In an AP, the difference between consecutive terms is:
quiz.explanation: A constant common difference is the entire definition of an AP — that's the one rule it never breaks.

quiz.question: The formula aₙ = a + (n - 1)d is used to find:
options: The nth term of an AP
quiz.explanation: This formula jumps straight to the nth term — no need to write out every term before it like it's 1990.

notes.title: Mean, Median, Mode
notes.content: Mean is the average of all observations, median is the middle value when data is arranged in order, and mode is the most frequently occurring value.

notes.title: Basic Probability
notes.content: Probability of an event = (Number of favourable outcomes) / (Total number of possible outcomes). Probability always lies between 0 and 1.

notes.title: Cumulative Frequency
notes.content: Cumulative frequency is the running total of frequencies up to a certain class interval, used to find the median in grouped data.

flashcards.front: Which measure of central tendency can get wrecked by one extreme outlier?
flashcards.back: Mean — one billionaire in the data and suddenly the 'average' income looks amazing.

flashcards.front: Can probability ever be greater than 1?
flashcards.back: Never. If your answer is above 1, you made an arithmetic mistake somewhere.
A running total of frequencies, used to find the median in grouped data.

quiz.question: Which of these values must always lie between 0 and 1?
quiz.explanation: Probability is capped between 0 (impossible) and 1 (certain) — no event is ever '150% likely,' no matter how confident you feel before an exam.

quiz.question: The most frequently occurring value in a data set is called the:
quiz.explanation: Mode is just the popularity contest winner of the data set — whichever value shows up the most.
